title: Human Resources Consulting Services

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541612 ($10,678,955,284) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541612 ($26,539,675,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $173,623 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541612); class <5: 5,881 firms, avg revenue/firm $394,584 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $61,752 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 651 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,244,538 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $194,770 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 412 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,382,619 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $372,880 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 431 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,933,174 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,241,542 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 144 firms, avg revenue/firm $29,228,493 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,574,259 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 122 firms, avg revenue/firm $121,304,852 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $18,984,209 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 575
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

ai_replaceable_share.source_fact: SHRM estimates that at least 50% of tasks are automated in 19.1% of HR jobs and reports a high of 27.2% for compensation, benefits, and job analysis specialists.
ai_replaceable_share.derivation: The cited source is US HR employment, not NAICS 541612 consulting firms and not task shares by the supplied parent-level SOC mix. I use its occupational evidence as a benchmark, then map repetitive analysis, drafting, administration and relationship/sign-off work to each supplied SOC. These are derived task-share judgments.

citations.title: Automation, Generative AI, and Job Displacement Risk in HR Employment
citations.figure_quoted: at least 50% of tasks are automated in 19.1% of HR jobs
citations.scope: SHRM 2025 US worker survey and May 2024 OEWS-based HR employment analysis; HR occupations, not NAICS 541612 consulting firms or a role-time study.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Research, benchmarking analysis, first-draft documentation and presentation preparation are exposed; executive advice and change facilitation remain.
Internal administration automates, while partner selling, senior client trust and accountable judgments remain.

breakdown_by_role.role: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Content, segmentation and campaign analysis automate, while positioning and relationship development persist.

breakdown_by_role.role: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Prospecting support and proposal drafting automate; complex advisory sales and retention remain human.
Status reporting, data collection and engagement administration automate; committee coordination remains.
Routine inquiries, scheduling and follow-up have high automation potential, with escalation retained.
The supplied operations-specialist role is analytical and process-heavy, but exceptions and client-specific interpretation remain.
AI assists coding and analytics tooling, while secure integration, testing and product accountability remain.
Reconciliation, filing preparation and repeat analysis automate, with review and regulated accountability retained.
Policy drafting, job-description and routine data work are exposed; sensitive advice and human decision-making remain.
Residual includes unlisted benefits, actuarial, junior-analysis and administration work; data/credential-gated work is less exposed than open advisory.

pi_dist.rationale: Project and retainer work can retain some productivity gains, while clients can benchmark effort-heavy analysis and drafting against AI-enabled alternatives.
pi_dist.source_fact: No fetched source established revenue mix or observed AI-era fee retention for $1-10M EBITDA US HR-consulting firms.
pi_dist.derivation: Judgmental midpoint reflecting project/retainer advisory plus protected data and credential work; no source establishes the fraction.

pi_moat.rationale: Recurring comp cycles, proprietary survey panels, credentials and privilege can protect some fees, but general policies and advice face HR-tech and AI self-service substitution.
pi_moat.source_fact: No fetched source quantified the protected-data/credential share of NAICS 541612 revenue or switching behavior after AI adoption.
pi_moat.derivation: Judgmental midpoint for a heterogeneous code; no source establishes the value.

t50_years.source_fact: SHRM reports 43% of organizations now leverage AI in HR tasks, up from 26% in 2024.
t50_years.derivation: The source measures client organizations' HR-task use, not US HR-consulting firms. I select 35% current consultant-firm material use, below the 43% broad-HR proxy, and a three-year path to 50% to allow procurement, confidentiality and workflow integration. This is a judgmental scope adjustment.

citations.title: The Role of AI in HR Continues to Expand
citations.figure_quoted: 43% of organizations now leverage AI in HR tasks, up from 26% in 2024
citations.scope: SHRM survey of 2,040 US HR professionals, fielded February 3-12, 2025; organizations across industries, not HR consulting firms in NAICS 541612.

current_adoption_pct.source_fact: SHRM reports: 43% of organizations now leverage AI in HR tasks; its sample contained 2,040 HR professionals from organizations across US industries.
current_adoption_pct.derivation: Target is US NAICS 541612 firms materially using AI in automatable functions, whereas SHRM measures client organizations using AI in HR tasks. I apply an 8-point downward scope adjustment from 43% to 35% for smaller fee-only and confidentiality-constrained consultancies; no source directly measures the target population.

citations.title: The Role of AI in HR Continues to Expand
citations.figure_quoted: 43% of organizations now leverage AI in HR tasks
citations.scope: SHRM survey of 2,040 US HR professionals, fielded February 3-12, 2025; broad employer adoption, not NAICS 541612 consulting-firm adoption.

historical_analogs.value: The relevant analogue is HR workflow automation: SHRM reports widespread AI use in routine recruiting support and notes that human judgment remains indispensable for cultural fit, soft skills and bias mitigation. It supports task assistance, not replacement of fee-based HR consulting.
historical_analogs.source_fact: SHRM states that just over half of organizations, 51%, use AI to support recruiting and that human intelligence remains indispensable for interpreting cultural fit, assessing soft skills and mitigating bias.
historical_analogs.derivation: The source is used only as a documented HR workflow analogue and boundary on human judgment; it is not converted into consulting-market revenue, ownership or deal evidence.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Routine-work automation is broad, but implications for consulting demand vary by proprietary data, credential and client-risk mix.

citations.title: The Role of AI in HR Continues to Expand
citations.figure_quoted: just over half of organizations (51%) using AI to support recruiting efforts
citations.scope: SHRM survey of US HR professionals; internal recruiting use, not HR consulting firm revenue or service substitution.

owners_60plus_pct.source_fact: No fetched code-specific owner-age dataset for HR consulting was found.
owners_60plus_pct.derivation: Estimate for professional-services boutiques; no source establishes a 60-plus owner share.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: No fetched source documented a NAICS 541612 succession shortage, so the formula flag is false.

active_consolidators.source_fact: No fetched source established a repeat-acquirer count limited to fee-based NAICS 541612 targets rather than brokerage, PEO, staffing or payroll services.
active_consolidators.derivation: Judgmental count after excluding adjacent-services activity. Live searches on 2026-07-20 for HR consulting acquisition fee based, human resources consulting services acquisition, and benefits consulting acquisition could not support an auditable NAICS-pure count.

buy_mult.source_fact: No fetched comparable source established EV/EBITDA entry multiples for $1-10M EBITDA fee-based HR-consulting targets after market-rate owner compensation.
buy_mult.derivation: Judgmental entry value for a diversified consulting target with some recurring work, without importing insurance-brokerage multiples; EV/EBITDA after market-rate owner compensation. No source establishes 4.0x.

exit_mult.source_fact: No fetched comparable source established exit EV/EBITDA multiples for a scaled fee-based NAICS 541612 platform after market-rate owner compensation.
exit_mult.derivation: Judgmental premium for recurring clients and potentially transferable data/process assets, conditional on avoiding brokerage-commission economics; EV/EBITDA after market-rate owner compensation. No source establishes 5.0x.

pricing_structure.value: The code is modeled as a mix of project fees, retainers, per-participant or per-plan technical work, and subscription-like survey/data products. No fetched source quantified this mix for the target EBITDA band.
pricing_structure.source_fact: No fetched source established NAICS 541612 revenue shares by fee type for US $1-10M EBITDA firms.
pricing_structure.derivation: Segment-model estimate informed by the service boundary; data-product and credential-gated work are treated separately from effort-benchmarked open advisory in capture judgments. No source establishes the mix.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Open advisory may dominate small firms; data/subscription and regulated benefits work may dominate selected specialist firms.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Deterministic uplift derives from role-level task fractions, while A uses a separate proxy-adjusted three-year firm-adoption path. Broad-HR adoption is below 50%, so t50 remains positive under v3.1.

terminal_value.justification: AI reduces cost in research, drafting, survey analysis and administration. Proprietary data, credentialed benefits/actuarial work, legal-risk work and recurring cycles preserve paid demand for part of the code, but generalist policies and advisory face HR-tech and client self-service substitution.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V and A draw only on HR task/adoption evidence and disclosed proxy bridges. Acquisition activity, consolidation and multiple assumptions were not used in either V or A.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Project and retainer advisory is more repriced when analysis/drafting hours fall, while proprietary survey/data and credentialed recurring work can retain more value. This maps to moderate pi_dist 0.50 and pi_moat 0.50 rather than treating all consulting revenue as equally protected.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges from 10.00 to 1.67 under the frozen formula.
The t50 boundary and A are sensitive to whether material consultant-firm adoption is already 50%; the direct source is a broad employer proxy.
C changes materially with the unmeasured mix of effort-benchmarked advisory versus protected data, credential and recurring revenue.

reviewer_note: DATASET_MISMATCH: supplied role_mix is 4-digit 5416 and management-consulting dominated; it omits distinctive compensation, benefits and actuarial roles. I used all supplied SOCs unchanged and one residual judgment. Heterogeneous boundary: dominant general HR/compensation advisory is scored; compensation-survey/data products, benefits/ERISA technical work and pay-equity/compliance work have different moats and exposure. Benefits brokerage, staffing, payroll, PEO and executive search are excluded but can contaminate market and M&A evidence.

V.arithmetic: Python finalizer: V_raw = labor_share × ai_replaceable_share = 0.429 × 0.463545 = 0.198860805; V = 10 × min(1, V_raw / 0.25) = 7.9544322

A.arithmetic: Python finalizer: A = 10 when t50_years = 0, otherwise min(10, 10 / t50_years); t50_years = 3, A = 3.33333333333

M.arithmetic: Python finalizer: M = clamp(4 × (exit_mult − buy_mult) / buy_mult, 0, 10) = 1
